title: Neuland capitals, Rudolf Koch's broad-nib alphabet
subtitle: Constructing the letterforms of Koch's 1923 display typeface with a flat nib
excerpt: Neuland is an all-capitals display alphabet designed by Rudolf Koch in 1923, built with a wide flat nib held at varying angles to produce thick, serif-free strokes of different widths. Each letter is made of a small number of large pen strokes, making it an unusually direct exercise in broad-nib control. The 1923 type designs are in the public domain.
sourceNotes: Rudolf Koch, Das Schreiben als Kunstfertigkeit (Writing as a Craft), Koch's letterform essays published in the 1920s. The Neuland typeface was released by Klingspor foundry in 1923 and the letterform designs entered the public domain in the EU and US. The Koch lettering tradition is documented in Fritz Kredel and George Salter, Rudolf Koch: The Little ABC Book (Neuland Schriften, 1976) and in earlier German craft lettering publications of the 1920s.

glossaryTerms.definition: An all-capitals display alphabet designed by Rudolf Koch in 1923. Broad, thick strokes with no serifs, constructed with a wide flat nib held at varying angles. Neuland is the typeface; the same letterforms can be produced by hand with a broad-edge nib.

The technique of deliberately shifting the angle of the pen nib during a stroke to change the stroke width. In Neuland, the nib is rotated between strokes to produce the characteristic variation in thickness across letters.
A letterform assembled from a planned sequence of separate pen strokes rather than written in a single flowing movement. Neuland, Roman capitals, and Uncial are all constructed alphabets.

content.text: was designed for display use, posters, book titles, headings, and was never intended to be written as a running hand. Koch worked at his table with a broad flat nib, constructing each letter from a few deliberate strokes. The original type designs are public domain; the letterforms themselves can be reproduced freely. What makes Neuland distinctive is the
: the nib is turned between strokes, so different parts of the same letter are different widths.

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, 0 (broadest)
items.substitutions: Brause Bandzug nib in the widest available size; both are broad-edge nibs

content.text: Use the broadest available nib, a Mitchell 0 gives approximately 5 mm across the writing face. The large nib produces the characteristic thick-and-thin contrast across a single letter. Work at a capital height of 3 to 4 nib-widths, which is lower than most broad-edge scripts; the low cap-height gives Neuland its squat, heavy presence.

Neuland uses three nib positions. At 0 degrees (the nib edge parallel to the baseline), a downstroke is at maximum width. At 45 degrees, it is at medium width. At 90 degrees (the nib edge perpendicular to the baseline), a downstroke is at hairline width. Different strokes within the same letter use different angles to create the characteristic variation.
Stroke 1: left vertical. Hold the nib at 45 degrees. Pull a straight vertical downstroke. This gives the left stem at medium width.
Stroke 2: the diagonal. Rotate the nib to 0 degrees (parallel to baseline). Pull a diagonal stroke from the top-left to the bottom-right. At 0 degrees the diagonal is at full width, thick and bold.
Stroke 3: right vertical. Return the nib to 45 degrees and pull the right stem. Both verticals match; the thick diagonal connects them.

The Neuland O is a wide, slightly flattened oval made in two strokes: a left half-oval and a right half-oval. Hold the nib at 0 degrees for both, the broad side of the nib runs along the curve, producing the characteristic 'brick-thick' oval with almost no variation in stroke weight around the curve. This is the opposite of Roman capitals, where the nib angle produces thin sides and thick top-and-bottom curves. The C is the left half-oval alone.

items.symptom: The letters look like ordinary italic capitals, not Neuland
items.cause: The nib angle is not being changed between strokes.
items.fix: Each stroke in Neuland requires a deliberate nib rotation. Keep a small diagram beside your work showing the nib angle required for each stroke type.

items.symptom: The ink floods or blobs at the start of each stroke
items.cause: Too much ink loaded; the broad nib holds a large reservoir.
items.fix: Blot the nib lightly before beginning each stroke. Load less ink overall.
